enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source radio automation and playout software for scheduling, streaming and managing terrestrial or internet radio stations.
enrichment.seo_title: LibreTime Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: LibreTime is a free, open-source radio automation platform for scheduling, playout and streaming. Explore features, hosting costs and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: LibreTime is a free and open-source radio automation and broadcast scheduling platform used by terrestrial, low-power FM, campus, community, and internet-only radio stations to plan, schedule, and play out audio content around the clock. The project traces its lineage to Airtime, a radio automation tool originally developed by the Prague-based non-profit technology company Sourcefabric starting around 2010 to serve community broadcasters worldwide. When Sourcefabric shifted its commercial focus toward a hosted SaaS product called Airtime Pro in 2016 and stopped actively developing the open-source version, a group of broadcasters and volunteer developers forked the codebase in 2017 and continued development under the new name LibreTime, building on unreleased work from Sourcefabric's own saas-dev branch. Since then, LibreTime has been maintained entirely by a volunteer community rather than a company, with governance, fundraising, and infrastructure coordinated through Open Collective and communication happening on a Discourse forum and Matrix chat rather than a corporate support channel. Architecturally, LibreTime is a multi-component system originally built primarily in PHP with a JavaScript front end, and its playout engine relies on Liquidsoap, the widely used audio-streaming scripting language, to handle the actual on-air mixing and transitions between scheduled tracks, live shows, and pre-recorded shows. The platform also includes a Python-based analyzer service that processes uploaded audio files for metadata and normalization, a REST API for integrating with other systems, and a web application for day-to-day station management. The whole stack is designed to be deployed with Docker Compose, which has made self-hosting considerably easier than in the Airtime era, when installation was notoriously fragile. The software is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0), meaning any organization that modifies LibreTime and offers it as a network service must publish its source changes, a copyleft protection intended to keep the ecosystem open even as commercial hosting providers offer managed LibreTime instances. Core features center on the day-to-day needs of a radio station's programming team: a drag-and-drop show scheduler and calendar for planning weekly and one-off broadcasts; a searchable audio library with waveform previews, tagging, and metadata editing; smart blocks and dynamic playlists that can auto-fill airtime based on rules such as genre or play count; support for live DJ input and remote broadcasting alongside pre-scheduled automation; podcast and RSS publishing so a station's on-air content can be repackaged for on-demand listening; multi-user accounts with role-based permissions for hosts, programmers, and administrators; and integration with Icecast and SHOUTcast for streaming the resulting signal online. Because it was built by and for broadcasters, LibreTime also supports the kind of overnight unattended automation that lets small stations with limited staff stay on air twenty-four hours a day. There is no proprietary pricing tier: LibreTime itself is entirely free to download, self-host, and modify. The cost a station actually incurs is for the server infrastructure needed to run it (a VPS or dedicated machine capable of running Docker, PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, and the streaming components) plus optional professional services. A small ecosystem of third-party hosts, such as Streamerr and Radio Hosting 24, offers fully managed LibreTime instances for stations that do not want to run their own servers, typically billed monthly, but this is a hosting fee rather than a software license fee, and users remain free to self-host at zero cost or migrate providers at any time since the underlying platform is identical. LibreTime's target audience is fundamentally mission-driven and budget-constrained broadcasting: community radio stations, university and high school radio programs, low-power FM licensees, non-profits, and hobbyist internet radio operators who need professional-grade scheduling and playout without the recurring licensing costs of commercial systems. Its most direct competitor is Sourcefabric's own Airtime Pro, the commercial hosted successor to the project LibreTime forked from, along with other automation tools such as RadioDJ, mAirList, Rivendell, and StationPlaylist, most of which are either Windows-only, closed-source, or priced per seat. LibreTime differentiates itself primarily through being free, auditable, and community-governed, with no vendor lock-in and a codebase that any technically capable station can inspect, self-host, and extend, at the cost of needing more in-house or contracted technical expertise than a fully managed commercial product would require.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: LibreTime is a free, open-source radio automation platform that grew out of a 2017 community fork of Sourcefabric's Airtime project, after Sourcefabric redirected its resources to a commercial hosted product called Airtime Pro and stopped actively developing the open-source version.

Maintained entirely by volunteers and fiscally supported through Open Collective, LibreTime gives terrestrial and internet radio stations a full scheduling, library, and playout system built on Liquidsoap, PostgreSQL, and a Docker-based deployment model, released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: LibreTime covers the core needs of a radio programming team: a drag-and-drop scheduler, a searchable and tagged audio library with waveform previews, smart blocks for rule-based dynamic playlists, and support for switching between automated playout and live or remote DJ input.

Stations can stream directly to Icecast or SHOUTcast for internet radio, republish on-air content as podcasts through RSS, and manage multiple staff accounts with role-based permissions, all accessible through a REST API for deeper integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: LibreTime carries no software licensing cost of any kind; the full feature set is free to self-host under AGPL-3.0. Costs are limited to the server infrastructure a station provisions to run it.

Stations that prefer not to self-host can pay a monthly fee to third-party managed hosting providers such as Streamerr or Radio Hosting 24, who run the identical open-source software as a paid service rather than a separate licensed product.

features.title: Automated Scheduling and Playout
features.description: Drag-and-drop calendar scheduling paired with a Liquidsoap-powered playout engine for gapless 24/7 automation.

features.title: Smart Blocks and Dynamic Playlists
features.description: Rule-based playlists that auto-fill airtime by genre, mood, or play count.

features.title: Web-Based Audio Library
features.description: Upload, tag, and search the station's audio library with waveform previews and metadata editing.

features.title: Live Show and Remote DJ Support
features.description: Switch seamlessly between scheduled automation and live or remote DJ input.

features.title: Podcast and RSS Publishing
features.description: Republish on-air shows as on-demand podcast feeds for listeners.

features.title: Icecast and SHOUTcast Streaming
features.description: Built-in integration to stream online alongside terrestrial broadcast.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Integrate LibreTime with station websites, mobile apps, and third-party tools.

features.title: Docker-Based Self-Hosting
features.description: Deploy via Docker Compose on your own infrastructure under the AGPL-3.0 license.

pros: Completely free and open source with no per-seat licensing fees
Actively maintained by a global volunteer community since 2017 with regular releases
AGPL license guarantees the code stays open even for hosted forks
Docker-based deployment is far easier than the older Airtime install process
Purpose-built for radio workflows like playlists, live shows, and overnight automation
cons: No official managed hosting from the project itself, so non-technical stations must find a third-party host or self-host
Documentation and support rely on community forums and chat rather than dedicated customer support
Interface and UX feel dated compared to modern commercial SaaS radio platforms
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance, backups, and security updates
Smaller contributor base than commercial competitors means some features and fixes move slowly

faqs.question: Is LibreTime really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core software is free and open source under AGPL-3.0; you only pay for the server or third-party hosting you choose to run it on.

faqs.question: What is the difference between LibreTime and Airtime?
faqs.answer: LibreTime is a 2017 community fork of Sourcefabric's open-source Airtime project, continued after Sourcefabric shifted focus to its commercial Airtime Pro SaaS product.

faqs.question: Can LibreTime stream to Icecast or SHOUTcast?
faqs.answer: Yes, LibreTime has built-in support for streaming to Icecast and SHOUTcast servers for internet radio.

faqs.question: Does LibreTime support live DJ shows as well as automation?
faqs.answer: Yes, stations can switch between scheduled automated playout and live or remote DJ input.

faqs.question: What are the system requirements to self-host LibreTime?
faqs.answer: LibreTime is deployed via Docker Compose and requires a server capable of running PostgreSQL, RabbitMQ, Liquidsoap, and the web application components.

faqs.question: Who maintains LibreTime?
faqs.answer: A volunteer, community-driven team maintains LibreTime, fiscally supported via Open Collective, rather than a commercial company.

use_cases.title: Community and Non-Profit Radio Automation
use_cases.description: Community stations use LibreTime to schedule shows, run 24/7 automation, and manage volunteer programmers without licensing costs.

use_cases.title: College and Campus Radio Stations
use_cases.description: University and high school radio programs use LibreTime to teach broadcasting operations while keeping software costs at zero.
use_cases.audience: University and high school radio programs

use_cases.title: Internet-Only and Low-Power FM Broadcasting
use_cases.description: Independent broadcasters self-host LibreTime to stream online via Icecast or run low-power FM automation.
use_cases.audience: Hobbyist and independent internet radio operators

features: Community forum and Matrix chat support
Provided by independent hosts such as Streamerr and Radio Hosting 24

enrichment.short_description: One of the oldest podcast hosting platforms, offering storage-tiered hosting, distribution and monetization for podcasters.
enrichment.seo_title: Libsyn Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Libsyn is the original podcast hosting platform, founded in 2004. Compare its storage-tiered pricing, monetization tools and best alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Libsyn, short for Liberated Syndication and formally Liberated Syndication Inc., is widely credited as the first dedicated podcast hosting company, launching in November 2004, more than six months before Apple added podcast support to iTunes. The company's roots go back to 1998, when University of Pittsburgh students David J. Chekan, Matthew T. Hoopes, Martin Mulligan, and David Mansueto started Emayhem, an online publishing collective for Pittsburgh-area artists and musicians, which evolved into a web design firm called Webmayhem in 2000. In 2004 the group began producing their own podcast and, to cut hosting costs, Mansueto stitched together a cluster of cheap servers into what was effectively a homemade content delivery network. That infrastructure became the first commercial podcast hosting platform, and the founders named the new business Liberated Syndication to reflect their goal of letting independent creators distribute audio freely. Early customers included NPR, comedian and podcaster Marc Maron, National Geographic, Grammar Girl, and then-Senator Barack Obama; a 2015 Obama interview hosted on Libsyn became the first podcast episode to top one million downloads within 24 hours. Libsyn was acquired by Wizzard Software Corporation in 2006, which later merged with Digital Entertainment International in 2012, before Libsyn was spun off again as an independent public company in 2016. It now trades on the OTCQB under the ticker LSYN and is headquartered at 5001 Baum Boulevard in Pittsburgh with a small, roughly 20-person corporate staff. The company diversified through acquisitions, buying web hosting provider Pair Networks for 16 million dollars in 2017 (divested to Your.Online in 2024), the ad-tech marketplace AdvertiseCast in 2019, and audio tools Auxbus and Glow FM in 2021. Reported revenue grew from 25.8 million dollars in 2020 to 58.7 million dollars in 2022, with advertising revenue via AdvertiseCast eventually accounting for the majority of the business. Functionally, Libsyn is a media-hosting and distribution service: creators upload episode audio, and Libsyn stores it, generates an RSS feed, and distributes that feed to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other directories with a single click, alongside a hosted podcast website, basic episode-level analytics with IAB-certified download statistics, and monetization tools. Rather than metering total storage or bandwidth, Libsyn's plans are priced by how much new audio a creator uploads each month, and back-catalog episodes remain hosted indefinitely at no extra cost on every plan, a structural choice that has defined its pricing model since the company's founding. Entry-level plans start at roughly 5 dollars a month for a small monthly upload allowance around 3 hours of compressed audio, scaling up through mid-tier plans around 15 to 20 dollars a month for larger allowances, up to professional and enterprise tiers priced from roughly 75 to 150 dollars a month or higher that add features like dynamic ad insertion, API access, a network-level RSS feed for podcast networks managing multiple shows, and dedicated account support. Most paid tiers include a free trial period. Through its AdvertiseCast subsidiary, Libsyn also operates a self-serve and managed advertising marketplace that connects podcasters with sponsors and can automatically insert host-read or programmatic ads into episodes, a significant differentiator from hosting-only competitors. Libsyn's target audience spans the full range of podcast creators, from individual hobbyists and independent shows to large media networks and public broadcasters who need reliable long-term hosting, industry-standard analytics for advertiser reporting, and integrated monetization. Its closest competitors include newer, more design-forward hosts such as Buzzsprout, Transistor, Captivate, and Simplecast, as well as Podbean and Spotify's free Spotify for Podcasters, which undercuts Libsyn on price for casual creators. Libsyn differentiates itself primarily on longevity and trust, being the platform many of the largest and longest-running shows have used since podcasting's earliest days, on its storage-forever guarantee for back catalogs, and on owning its own advertising marketplace rather than relying solely on third-party ad networks, though its interface and per-plan upload limits are frequently cited by reviewers as more dated and less generous than newer entrants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Libsyn launched in November 2004 in Pittsburgh, evolving out of an earlier arts-collective and web-design venture, and is generally regarded as the first dedicated podcast hosting company, predating Apple's iTunes podcast support by over six months.

Now publicly traded as Liberated Syndication Inc. (OTCQB: LSYN), Libsyn has grown through acquisitions including AdvertiseCast, Auxbus, and Glow FM, evolving from a pure hosting service into a hosting-plus-monetization platform for podcasters of all sizes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Libsyn provides RSS-based hosting and one-click distribution to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and Amazon Music, along with a hosted podcast website and IAB-certified download analytics for advertiser reporting.
Its AdvertiseCast subsidiary adds a sponsorship marketplace and dynamic ad insertion, letting eligible shows monetize directly through the platform rather than relying on a separate ad network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Libsyn prices plans by how much new audio is uploaded each month rather than total storage, with back-catalog episodes hosted forever at no extra cost on every tier, starting around 5 dollars a month.

Higher tiers scale up through roughly 15 to 20 dollars a month for larger creators, with professional and enterprise pricing from about 75 to 150 dollars a month adding dynamic ad insertion, API access, and network-level RSS management.

features.title: RSS Feed Hosting and Distribution
features.description: Automatic distribution of a show's RSS feed to Apple Podcasts, Spotify, Amazon Music, and other directories.

features.title: Storage-Tiered Pricing with Unlimited Back Catalog
features.description: Plans are priced by monthly new-upload allowance while older episodes stay hosted forever.

features.title: IAB-Certified Analytics
features.description: Industry-standard download statistics suitable for advertiser reporting.

features.title: AdvertiseCast Ad Marketplace
features.description: Built-in sponsorship marketplace and dynamic ad insertion for eligible shows.

features.title: Hosted Podcast Website
features.description: A ready-made website generated automatically for each show.

features.title: One-Click Directory Distribution
features.description: Publish once and syndicate automatically to major podcast apps and platforms.

features.title: Podcast Network Management
features.description: Enterprise tools for managing multiple shows under a single network-level RSS feed.

features.title: Mobile Recording and Publishing App
features.description: Record and publish episodes directly from a mobile device.

pros: One of the longest operating histories in podcasting, in business since 2004
Back-catalog episodes stay hosted forever with no storage cap on any plan
Built-in AdvertiseCast marketplace for sponsorships and dynamic ad insertion
Publicly traded company (LSYN) with audited financials and long-term stability
Proven at scale, having hosted some of the largest podcasts in the industry
cons: Upload-based pricing tiers can feel restrictive for frequent or long-form publishers compared to flat-fee competitors
Interface and dashboard are often described as dated next to newer hosts
Entry-level plans offer relatively small monthly upload allowances
Fewer built-in modern creator tools such as transcription or AI show notes than some newer competitors
Customer support quality has drawn mixed reviews compared to boutique hosting competitors

faqs.question: When was Libsyn founded?
faqs.answer: Libsyn traces back to 1998 as an online arts collective and launched as the Liberated Syndication podcast hosting service in November 2004.

faqs.question: Is Libsyn a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Libsyn's parent company, Liberated Syndication Inc., trades on the OTCQB under the ticker LSYN.

faqs.question: How does Libsyn pricing work?
faqs.answer: Plans are priced by how much new audio you upload each month rather than total storage, and previously published episodes remain hosted at no extra charge.

faqs.question: Does Libsyn help with podcast monetization?
faqs.answer: Yes, through its AdvertiseCast subsidiary Libsyn offers a sponsorship marketplace and dynamic ad insertion for eligible shows.

faqs.question: Where does Libsyn distribute podcasts?
faqs.answer: Libsyn automatically distributes a show's RSS feed to major directories including Apple Podcasts, Spotify, and Amazon Music.

faqs.question: What happened to Libsyn's web hosting business?
faqs.answer: Libsyn acquired web host Pair Networks in 2017 and sold it to Your.Online in 2024 to refocus on podcasting.

use_cases.title: Podcast Networks and Media Companies

use_cases.title: Advertiser-Supported Shows

features: Roughly 3 hours of new upload storage per month
Roughly 6 hours of new upload storage per month
Roughly 10 hours of new upload storage per month
Unlimited or very high upload storage
Network-level RSS feed for multiple shows

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source music collection manager that automatically finds, downloads and organizes albums, part of the *arr suite.
enrichment.seo_title: Lidarr Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lidarr is a free, open-source music collection manager that automates album downloads and organization. See features, setup and top *arr alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lidarr is a free, open-source music collection manager created in May 2017 by a community of developers connected to the Servarr project family, the loose collective of self-hosted media-automation tools that also produces Sonarr for TV shows, Radarr for movies, Readarr for books, Prowlarr for indexer management, and Bazarr for subtitles. Lidarr was built directly on Sonarr's codebase and design philosophy, adapted for music, and its own documentation describes it as looking and behaving like Sonarr but made for music. Like the rest of the *arr suite, Lidarr has no company behind it and no commercial entity; it is maintained by volunteer contributors on GitHub under the Lidarr organization, released under the GNU General Public License version 3, and distributed for free to anyone running a home media server or self-hosted download automation stack. Development is active, with well over ten thousand commits and more than a hundred tagged releases, and the project's primary support channels are Discord and GitHub issues rather than a formal helpdesk. At its core, Lidarr connects to a user's preferred download clients, such as SABnzbd, NZBGet, qBittorrent, Deluge, or Transmission, and to indexer-management tools like Prowlarr or the older Jackett, to automate the process of building and maintaining a music library. A user adds an artist to Lidarr, and the application then monitors that artist for new releases via RSS feeds and periodic searches, automatically finding and grabbing matching albums according to a configurable quality profile, for example preferring lossless FLAC over MP3, or a specific bitrate. Once a file is downloaded, Lidarr renames and organizes it into a folder structure the user defines, retrieves metadata such as album art, track listings, and artist information from the MusicBrainz database, and can automatically replace an existing file with a higher-quality version if one becomes available later, a feature commonly called automatic quality upgrading. Lidarr also handles failed or stalled downloads by automatically searching for an alternative release, supports manual search and interactive download selection when automation is not desired, and integrates with media servers such as Plex, Jellyfin, Kodi, and Navidrome so that newly downloaded music becomes streamable immediately after import. The application runs cross-platform, with official support for Windows, Linux, macOS, and ARM devices like the Raspberry Pi, and is very commonly deployed as a Docker container alongside the rest of a self-hosted *arr media stack. Because Lidarr is entirely open source, there is no pricing model or paid tier of any kind; the software itself, its source code, and all of its features are free to download and self-host indefinitely, and users who prefer not to manage their own server can rent a pre-configured Lidarr instance from third-party self-hosting providers such as ElfHosted, which charge a hosting fee rather than a software license. Costs a user typically incurs are indirect: server or NAS hardware, a Usenet or torrent indexer subscription, and a VPN if desired for torrent traffic, none of which are billed by the Lidarr project itself. Lidarr's target audience is technically inclined home-media enthusiasts, often the same people running Plex or Jellyfin media servers, who want a Sonarr-like automated workflow specifically for music rather than manually searching for and tagging albums. Within the broader *arr ecosystem it sits alongside Sonarr and Radarr as one of the three most widely deployed applications, and it is frequently paired with Prowlarr for centralized indexer management. Outside the *arr family, its nearest functional relatives are library-tagging tools like Beets and MusicBrainz Picard, which handle metadata and organization for files a user has already acquired but do not automate discovery and downloading the way Lidarr does, and streaming-focused personal music servers like Navidrome or Airsonic, which consume the library Lidarr builds rather than building it. Lidarr differentiates itself by owning the acquisition and organization layer specifically for music, filling the same role for audio collections that Sonarr and Radarr fill for television and film in a self-hosted media automation pipeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lidarr is a free, open-source music collection manager launched in May 2017 as part of the Servarr family of self-hosted media automation tools, built on the same codebase and design philosophy as Sonarr but adapted specifically for music.

With no company behind it, Lidarr is maintained entirely by volunteer contributors under the GPL-3.0 license and is typically deployed via Docker alongside Sonarr, Radarr, and Prowlarr as part of a broader self-hosted media stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lidarr monitors artists for new releases via RSS and periodic searches, automatically grabbing matching albums according to configurable quality profiles and retrieving metadata and album art from MusicBrainz.

It integrates with download clients such as SABnzbd and qBittorrent and with media servers like Plex, Jellyfin, Kodi, and Navidrome, and can automatically upgrade existing files when higher-quality versions become available.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lidarr is entirely free and open source, with no paid tiers, license fees, or feature restrictions of any kind for self-hosted use.

Users who do not want to manage their own server can rent a pre-configured Lidarr instance from third-party self-hosting providers such as ElfHosted, which charge a hosting fee rather than a software license.

features.title: Artist Monitoring and RSS Automation
features.description: Automatically watches monitored artists for new album releases across configured indexers.

features.title: Quality Profiles and Automatic Upgrades
features.description: Grabs releases matching a configured quality profile and upgrades files as better versions appear.

features.title: MusicBrainz Metadata and Album Art
features.description: Retrieves accurate album art, track listings, and artist metadata automatically.

features.title: Download Client Integration
features.description: Works with SABnzbd, NZBGet, qBittorrent, Deluge, and Transmission.

features.title: Custom File Renaming and Organization
features.description: Organizes downloaded music into a user-defined folder and naming structure.

features.title: Failed Download Handling
features.description: Automatically searches for an alternative release when a download fails or stalls.

features.title: Media Server Integration
features.description: Syncs with Plex, Jellyfin, Kodi, and Navidrome for immediate streaming after import.

features.title: Cross-Platform, Docker-Ready Deployment
features.description: Runs on Windows, Linux, macOS, and ARM devices including the Raspberry Pi.

pros: Completely free and open source with no license fees
Fully automates finding, downloading, and tagging new music releases
Tight integration with the rest of the *arr suite and Prowlarr
Active community development with frequent releases
Runs on almost any hardware, including a Raspberry Pi, via Docker
cons: Requires technical setup, including configuring indexers and download clients
No official cloud-hosted version, so most users must self-host
No formal customer support, only community Discord and GitHub
Relies on the availability of releases on the user's chosen indexers
Can raise legal and content-licensing considerations depending on indexer sources

faqs.question: Is Lidarr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lidarr is completely free and open-source software under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: What is the *arr suite?
faqs.answer: The *arr suite is a family of open-source, self-hosted media automation tools, including Sonarr for TV, Radarr for movies, Readarr for books, and Lidarr for music.

faqs.question: Does Lidarr download music itself?
faqs.answer: Lidarr does not download files directly; it searches indexers via Prowlarr or Jackett and sends grabs to a download client such as SABnzbd or qBittorrent.

faqs.question: Can Lidarr upgrade existing music quality automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lidarr can automatically replace a file with a higher-quality version when one matching your quality profile becomes available.

faqs.question: What media servers work with Lidarr?
faqs.answer: Lidarr integrates with Plex, Jellyfin, Kodi, and Navidrome, among others, for streaming the organized library.

faqs.question: Can I run Lidarr on a Raspberry Pi?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lidarr supports Windows, Linux, macOS, and ARM devices including the Raspberry Pi, and is commonly run via Docker.

use_cases.title: Home Media Server Automation
use_cases.description: Self-hosters use Lidarr alongside Plex or Jellyfin to keep a music library automatically updated.

use_cases.title: Personal Music Library Curation
use_cases.description: Audiophiles use Lidarr's quality profiles to build and maintain large, consistently tagged FLAC or MP3 collections.

use_cases.title: Automated Usenet/Torrent Music Acquisition
use_cases.description: Power users pair Lidarr with Prowlarr and a download client to fully automate music acquisition.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosted power users running a *arr stack

features: Docker, Windows, Linux, and macOS support

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, AI-enabled business intelligence platform built natively on top of dbt for governed self-serve analytics.
enrichment.seo_title: Lightdash Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lightdash is an open-source BI platform built on dbt. Compare its self-hosted and Cloud Pro pricing, AI agent features, and top Looker or Metabase alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lightdash is an open-source business intelligence platform designed to sit directly on top of a dbt (data build tool) project, letting analytics engineers define metrics once in code and letting the rest of an organization explore, chart, and monitor those metrics without needing to write SQL. The company was founded in 2020 in London by Hamzah Chaudhary and Oliver Laslett, who had previously worked together on the data team at UK insurtech company Cytora, where they grew frustrated that the metrics and data-quality logic they built were only useful when re-implemented inside whichever BI tool the business happened to use. The company entered Y Combinator's Summer 2020 batch under the original name Hubble, initially focused on automatically testing data warehouses for data-quality issues, before pivoting the product and rebranding to Lightdash in 2021 to focus specifically on a dbt-native BI layer. The UK legal entity behind the product is registered as Telescope Technology Ltd. In October 2022 Lightdash launched its commercial cloud offering and disclosed 8.4 million dollars in funding, including a seed round led by Accel with participation from Moonfire, Y Combinator, Snyk founder Guy Podjarny, and former GitLab CMO Ashley Kramer. In October 2024 the company raised an additional 11 million dollars led by Accel to fund an expansion into AI-driven analytics, introducing AI agents that can build charts and dashboards and answer natural-language questions inside tools like Slack, repositioning the product as agentic BI rather than a traditional dashboarding tool. Architecturally, Lightdash's core idea is to treat a dbt project's YAML-defined models as the single source of truth for business metrics and dimensions, generating a semantic layer directly from that code rather than requiring a separate, duplicated modeling step inside the BI tool itself, the way Looker's LookML or a typical drag-and-drop BI tool would. Analysts and business users then use Lightdash's web interface to explore these governed metrics, build charts and dashboards, run ad hoc queries through a built-in SQL runner, schedule recurring report deliveries, and configure alerts on metric thresholds. On top of this, Lightdash has layered AI agents capable of assembling charts and answering data questions in natural language, both inside the Lightdash app and via Slack integration, aiming to give less technical stakeholders self-serve access to the same governed metrics analysts rely on. Lightdash offers three pricing tiers. The Open Source tier is free and self-hosted, giving any team the full core feature set, including the dbt integration, data explorer, report builder, scheduled deliveries, alerting, and SQL runner, with only community Slack support. Cloud Pro is Lightdash's hosted commercial offering, priced at 3,000 dollars a month with no per-seat charges, meaning an organization can add unlimited users without incremental licensing cost, and it unlocks the AI agent features not available in the open-source tier. The Enterprise tier is custom-priced and adds capabilities aimed at larger, more regulated organizations, including custom roles, SSO with SAML and SCIM 2.0 provisioning, and a HIPAA business-associate-agreement option priced separately at 490 dollars a month, with deployment available in the cloud or on-premises. For companies embedding Lightdash-powered analytics into their own products, Lightdash also offers usage-based embedding pricing, with the first 1,000 chart loads free and 0.05 dollars per load after that, or a flat predictable rate of 790 dollars a month per 100,000 loads through what it calls an Embed Worker. A 21-day free trial is typically offered for the Cloud Pro tier. Lightdash's target audience is data teams that have already standardized on dbt for data transformation and want their BI layer to inherit that investment rather than duplicate metric logic in a second proprietary modeling language, spanning data-driven startups through to larger analytics engineering organizations that value open-source auditability alongside a governed semantic layer. Its most direct competitors are Looker, whose LookML semantic-layer approach Lightdash explicitly positions itself against, along with Mode, Metabase, Apache Superset, Omni Analytics, Hex, ThoughtSpot, and headless semantic-layer tools like Cube. Lightdash differentiates itself chiefly through being open source and dbt-native from the ground up rather than adding a dbt integration on top of a general-purpose BI tool, its no-per-seat pricing model that removes the incentive to restrict access to a small group of licensed analysts, and its more recent pivot toward AI agents that can operate directly on the governed semantic layer rather than hallucinating over raw tables, an approach it markets as reducing the risk of AI-generated analytics giving wrong answers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lightdash is an open-source BI platform founded in 2020 in London by Hamzah Chaudhary and Oliver Laslett, originally launched through Y Combinator under the name Hubble before pivoting to a dbt-native BI product and rebranding in 2021.

Backed by Accel and Y Combinator with 8.4 million dollars in early funding and an additional 11 million dollars raised in 2024, Lightdash builds its semantic layer directly from a team's existing dbt project rather than requiring a separate proprietary modeling layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lightdash's data explorer and report builder let business users chart and analyze governed dbt metrics without writing SQL, supported by a built-in SQL runner, scheduled deliveries, and metric alerting.

Since 2024 Lightdash has added AI agents that can build charts, answer natural-language data questions, and respond inside Slack, positioning the product as agentic BI built on top of a governed semantic layer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lightdash offers a fully-featured free, open-source, self-hosted tier alongside a hosted Cloud Pro plan priced at 3,000 dollars a month with no per-seat charges, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier with SSO, SAML, SCIM 2.0, and optional HIPAA support.

Companies embedding Lightdash analytics into their own products can choose pay-as-you-go pricing at 0.05 dollars per chart load after the first 1,000 free loads, or a flat 790 dollars a month per 100,000 loads.

features.title: Native dbt Integration
features.description: Generates a governed semantic layer directly from an existing dbt project's YAML models.

features.title: Self-Serve Data Explorer and Report Builder
features.description: Lets non-technical users build charts and dashboards from governed metrics without SQL.

features.title: AI Agents for Natural-Language Analytics
features.description: AI agents can build charts and answer data questions in plain language.

features.title: Scheduled Deliveries and Metric Alerting
features.description: Automates recurring report delivery and threshold-based alerts.

features.title: Built-In SQL Runner
features.description: Lets analysts run ad hoc SQL queries directly inside the platform.

features.title: No Per-Seat Pricing
features.description: Cloud Pro and Enterprise plans support unlimited users without incremental licensing cost.

features.title: Slack Integration
features.description: AI agents can answer data questions directly inside Slack.

features.title: Open Source Self-Hosting
features.description: Full core feature set available for free under a self-hosted open-source deployment.

pros: Metrics are defined once in dbt and reused across the BI layer, avoiding duplicated logic
Genuinely free, full-featured open-source self-hosted option
No per-seat pricing on Cloud Pro removes the cost penalty for wide adoption
Backed by Accel and Y Combinator with continued investment in AI features
Governed semantic layer reduces the risk of inconsistent metric definitions across teams
cons: Requires an existing dbt project, so teams without dbt face a steeper setup
Cloud Pro's flat 3,000 dollars a month entry price is a significant jump from the free tier
Smaller ecosystem and third-party integrations than incumbents like Looker
AI agent features are reserved for paid Cloud Pro and Enterprise tiers
Enterprise-grade compliance features like SSO and SAML require the custom-priced Enterprise plan

faqs.question: Is Lightdash free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lightdash offers a fully-featured free, open-source, self-hosted tier, alongside a paid Cloud Pro plan starting at 3,000 dollars a month.

faqs.question: Does Lightdash require dbt?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lightdash is built to sit on top of an existing dbt project and generates its semantic layer directly from dbt models.

faqs.question: Who founded Lightdash?
faqs.answer: Lightdash was founded in 2020 by Hamzah Chaudhary and Oliver Laslett, initially launching through Y Combinator under the name Hubble.

faqs.question: Does Lightdash charge per user?
faqs.answer: No, Lightdash's Cloud Pro and Enterprise plans do not use per-seat pricing, allowing unlimited users.

faqs.question: What AI features does Lightdash offer?
faqs.answer: Lightdash offers AI agents that can build charts, answer natural-language data questions, and respond inside Slack, available on Cloud Pro and Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: How is Lightdash different from Looker?
faqs.answer: Unlike Looker's proprietary LookML modeling layer, Lightdash generates its semantic layer directly from an existing dbt project, avoiding duplicated metric definitions.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based restaurant point-of-sale system from Lightspeed Commerce for order management, payments and multi-location operations.
enrichment.seo_title: Lightspeed Restaurant Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lightspeed Restaurant is Lightspeed Commerce's cloud POS for restaurants. Compare Starter, Essential and Premium pricing, features and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lightspeed Restaurant is the restaurant-focused point-of-sale product line of Lightspeed Commerce Inc., a publicly traded Canadian commerce technology company founded in 2005 in Montreal by Dax Dasilva. Dasilva originally built the first Lightspeed product as a Mac-native point-of-sale system for independent retailers who wanted an alternative to clunky Windows-based systems, and the company grew over the following decade into a broader commerce platform spanning both retail and hospitality. Lightspeed went public on the Toronto Stock Exchange in March 2019 in what the Financial Post described as the most successful Canadian technology IPO in nearly a decade, and it now also trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker LSPD. Dasilva stepped down as CEO in February 2022 and returned to the role in February 2024. The modern Lightspeed Restaurant product was built substantially through acquisition: Lightspeed acquired the Rhode Island-based restaurant management platform Upserve in 2018 and the Australian point-of-sale company Kounta in 2019, folding their technology and teams into what is now marketed as Lightspeed Restaurant, alongside the company's earlier restaurant offerings. By fiscal 2025 to 2026, Lightspeed Commerce employed roughly 3,000 people worldwide across its retail, restaurant, and golf hospitality product lines, serving well over 150,000 customer locations globally, with restaurants representing one of its two primary verticals alongside retail. As a product, Lightspeed Restaurant is a cloud-based, tablet and terminal-driven POS built around the daily operational needs of independent restaurants, bars, cafes, and multi-location hospitality groups. Its core toolset includes a customizable point-of-sale interface, digital menu management, visual floor-plan and table management, order and pay-at-table functionality, online and contactless ordering, and integrated CRM and loyalty tools for repeat-guest marketing. Higher tiers add multi-location management, deeper ingredient-level inventory tracking with real-time stock alerts, a raw API for custom integrations, and support for hotel and hospitality operations with multiple revenue centers. The companion Lightspeed Pulse mobile app lets owners and managers monitor sales and operations remotely from a phone, and the platform includes Lightspeed AI-branded reporting and insights features across its plans. A kitchen display system is available as an add-on across all tiers at 30 dollars per screen per month. Pricing is structured in three self-serve tiers plus a custom Enterprise plan. The Starter tier is priced at 69 dollars a month and covers core POS functionality, menu and floor-plan management, integrated payments, and online and contactless ordering, positioned for single-location, simpler operations. The Essential tier, at 189 dollars a month, adds multi-location management, the Lightspeed Pulse app, and more advanced payment configuration options, aimed at growing restaurants with expectations of a fuller-service guest experience. The Premium tier, at 399 dollars a month, unlocks raw API access, advanced inventory management, and support for multiple revenue centers, targeting larger, more complex hospitality operations such as hotels or restaurant groups. An Enterprise tier above that is fully custom-priced and bundles personalized software and hardware packages with a dedicated customer success manager and unlimited onboarding and consultation. Payment processing runs through Lightspeed Payments, Lightspeed's own integrated processor, at a standard rate of 2.6 percent plus 10 cents per in-person transaction; merchants who choose a third-party processor instead of Lightspeed Payments are typically charged an additional monthly non-integrated-payments fee. Support includes 24/7 phone availability across plans, which the company positions as a differentiator for restaurants that need help during service. Lightspeed Restaurant's target customers range from single-location independent restaurants and bars to multi-location hospitality groups and hotels needing a unified POS across food and beverage outlets. Its primary competitors include Toast, the market-leading US restaurant POS built specifically for the category; Square for Restaurants, which appeals to smaller and lower-volume operators already using Square's broader payments ecosystem; Clover; TouchBistro; and Revel Systems. Lightspeed differentiates itself through being part of a larger public multi-vertical commerce company with retail, restaurant, and golf products under one roof, giving multi-concept operators a single vendor relationship, along with an emphasis on deep table and floor-plan management inherited from its Upserve and Kounta acquisitions, though reviewers frequently note that total cost of ownership, once payment processing, hardware, and add-ons like the kitchen display system are included, tends to land meaningfully higher than the sticker price of any individual tier, often in the 300 to 700 dollar per month range for a typical small restaurant.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lightspeed Restaurant is the food-service point-of-sale product from Lightspeed Commerce Inc. (NYSE and TSX: LSPD), a publicly traded commerce technology company founded in 2005 in Montreal by Dax Dasilva.

The current product was built substantially through the 2018 acquisition of Upserve and the 2019 acquisition of Kounta, and now serves single-location restaurants through multi-location hospitality groups and hotels.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lightspeed Restaurant covers menu management, visual floor plans and table management, pay-at-table and contactless ordering, and CRM and loyalty tools, with higher tiers adding multi-location management and ingredient-level inventory tracking.

The companion Lightspeed Pulse mobile app lets owners monitor performance remotely, and Premium-tier customers get raw API access along with support for multiple revenue centers in hotel and hospitality settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lightspeed Restaurant is priced in three tiers: Starter at 69 dollars a month, Essential at 189 dollars a month, and Premium at 399 dollars a month, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier for large or complex operators.

Lightspeed Payments charges 2.6 percent plus 10 cents per in-person transaction and is effectively the default processor, while a kitchen display system is billed separately at 30 dollars per screen per month.

Pricing and feature tiers can be complex to compare against flat-rate competitors like Square

faqs.question: How much does Lightspeed Restaurant cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at 69 dollars a month for Starter, rising to 189 for Essential and 399 for Premium, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier above that.

faqs.question: Who owns Lightspeed Restaurant?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Restaurant is a product of Lightspeed Commerce Inc., a publicly traded Canadian company (NYSE and TSX: LSPD) founded in 2005.

faqs.question: Does Lightspeed Restaurant require its own payment processor?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Payments is the default processor at 2.6 percent plus 10 cents per transaction, and using a third-party processor typically incurs an extra monthly fee.

faqs.question: Can Lightspeed Restaurant handle multiple locations?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-location management is available starting on the Essential plan.

faqs.question: What acquisitions built Lightspeed Restaurant?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Restaurant incorporates technology from Upserve, acquired in 2018, and Kounta, acquired in 2019.

faqs.question: Does Lightspeed Restaurant support online ordering?
faqs.answer: Yes, online ordering and contactless ordering are included starting on the Starter plan.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud-based retail point-of-sale and inventory management system from Lightspeed Commerce, distinct from Lightspeed Restaurant.
enrichment.seo_title: Lightspeed Retail Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lightspeed Retail is Lightspeed Commerce's cloud POS for retailers. Compare Basic, Core and Plus pricing, features and top retail POS alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lightspeed Retail is the retail-focused point-of-sale and inventory management product from Lightspeed Commerce Inc., the same publicly traded Montreal company (NYSE and TSX: LSPD) behind Lightspeed Restaurant, but built and sold as a distinct product for shops, boutiques, and multi-location retail chains rather than food-service businesses. Lightspeed Commerce was founded in 2005 by Dax Dasilva, who built the company's original product as a Mac-based point-of-sale system for independent retailers, which today is retained in the market as Lightspeed's legacy R-Series. Over the following two decades Lightspeed expanded aggressively through acquisition on the retail side, most significantly acquiring the US-based iPad POS company ShopKeep in 2020 for roughly 440 million dollars and the New Zealand-founded cloud retail POS company Vend in 2021 for approximately 425 million dollars. Vend's cloud-native architecture became the technical foundation for what Lightspeed now sells as Lightspeed Retail X-Series, the current flagship retail product and the one most commonly referred to simply as Lightspeed Retail, existing alongside the older, more hardware-dependent R-Series for merchants who have not migrated. Lightspeed went public on the Toronto Stock Exchange in 2019 and now employs roughly 3,000 people worldwide across its retail, restaurant, and golf hospitality (Lightspeed Golf) divisions. Functionally, Lightspeed Retail X-Series is a cloud-based POS and back-office system covering the operational needs of a retail business: point-of-sale checkout that works online or offline, inventory management with catalogs of more than 8 million pre-loaded products for faster item setup, multi-location stock tracking with the ability to see and transfer inventory across stores, product variant support such as size and color attribute matrices, integrated vendor catalogs for reordering stock, built-in ecommerce so a retailer's online store shares inventory and orders with the physical POS in real time, customer loyalty programs, advanced sales and inventory reporting, and a mobile scanner app for stocktaking and receiving without dedicated hardware. Higher tiers add a retail API for building custom integrations and workflows, and Single Sign-On for larger organizations managing employee access across many locations. Payment processing is handled through Lightspeed Payments at a card-present rate as low as 1.5 percent for qualifying transactions, though merchants can alternatively use select third-party processors. Pricing is tiered into three self-serve plans plus a custom Enterprise option, each covering a single register, with additional registers and locations priced on request. The Basic plan is 89 dollars a month and covers the essential day-to-day POS and inventory needs of an independent retailer. The Core plan, at 149 dollars a month, is aimed at retailers who want growth-oriented management tools such as deeper reporting and loyalty features. The Plus plan, at 289 dollars a month, is built for more established, multi-location retail operations and is the tier that unlocks the retail API for custom integrations and advanced customization. An Enterprise tier above Plus is custom-priced and built around large retail chains' specific requirements. All tiers include 24 hour chat and phone support. Lightspeed periodically runs promotional first-year discounts on these list prices. Lightspeed Retail's target customers are independent boutiques, specialty shops, and small-to-midsize retail chains across categories like apparel, home goods, bike shops, and sporting goods, particularly those that need combined in-store and online inventory management rather than the food-service-specific tools of Lightspeed Restaurant. It is important not to confuse the two products: Lightspeed Restaurant is built around menus, tables, kitchen workflows, and food-service payment flows, while Lightspeed Retail is built around SKU-level inventory, product catalogs, and omnichannel retail selling, and a hospitality business should not be sized against Lightspeed Retail's feature set or pricing. Lightspeed Retail's main competitors are Square for Retail, Shopify POS, Clover Retail, and Epos Now, with Vend itself having been folded into and effectively retired as a standalone brand in favor of the unified Lightspeed Retail X-Series naming after the 2021 acquisition. Lightspeed differentiates itself in this category through the depth of its multi-location inventory tools, its very large pre-built product catalog for faster onboarding, and native ecommerce integration that keeps online and in-store stock synchronized, at the cost of a higher starting price than some flat-rate competitors like Square.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lightspeed Retail is the retail-specific point-of-sale product from Lightspeed Commerce Inc. (NYSE and TSX: LSPD), distinct from the company's separate Lightspeed Restaurant product for food service.

The current X-Series product is built on technology acquired with Vend in 2021, following the 2020 acquisition of ShopKeep, and exists alongside Lightspeed's older, Mac-based legacy R-Series retail product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lightspeed Retail covers cloud POS checkout, multi-location inventory tracking, a catalog of more than 8 million pre-loaded products, and built-in ecommerce that syncs online and in-store stock in real time.
Higher tiers add a retail API for custom workflows and Single Sign-On for larger organizations, alongside customer loyalty tools and a mobile scanner app for stocktaking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lightspeed Retail is priced in three self-serve tiers covering a single register each: Basic at 89 dollars a month, Core at 149 dollars a month, and Plus at 289 dollars a month, plus a custom Enterprise tier for large chains.
Lightspeed Payments offers card-present rates as low as 1.5 percent for qualifying retail transactions, and additional registers or locations are priced on request rather than listed publicly.

Frequently confused with sibling product Lightspeed Restaurant despite serving a different market

faqs.question: What is the difference between Lightspeed Retail and Lightspeed Restaurant?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Retail is built for shops and inventory-driven retail businesses, while Lightspeed Restaurant is built for food-service operations with menus, tables and kitchen workflows; both are separate products from the same parent company, Lightspeed Commerce.

faqs.question: How much does Lightspeed Retail cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at 89 dollars a month for Basic, rising to 149 for Core and 289 for Plus, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier above that.

faqs.question: Is Lightspeed Retail the same as Vend?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Retail's current X-Series product is built on the technology Lightspeed acquired when it purchased Vend in 2021.

faqs.question: Does Lightspeed Retail include ecommerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, built-in ecommerce that syncs inventory with the physical POS is included across plans.

faqs.question: What payment processing does Lightspeed Retail use?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Payments offers card-present rates as low as 1.5 percent for qualifying retail transactions, with third-party processors also available.

faqs.question: Who owns Lightspeed Retail?
faqs.answer: Lightspeed Retail is owned by Lightspeed Commerce Inc. (NYSE and TSX: LSPD), a publicly traded company founded in 2005 in Montreal.

enrichment.short_description: Lila is the open-source codebase powering Lichess.org, the free, ad-free, nonprofit online chess platform.
enrichment.company_size: Small (paid core team plus volunteer contributors)
enrichment.seo_title: Lila (Lichess) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lila is the open-source code behind Lichess.org, the free nonprofit chess platform. Explore its features, nonprofit funding model and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lila is the name of the open-source software codebase that powers Lichess.org, one of the largest online chess platforms in the world; the name is a contraction of lichess in Scala, the primary programming language the backend is written in. Lichess was created in 2010 by French programmer Thibault Duplessis, who started the project as a personal experiment in real-time web communication, building a simple online chessboard that, in his own account, did not even validate legal moves at first. The project found an audience almost immediately, and Duplessis, joined over time by a growing group of volunteer developers, translators, and moderators, built it out into a full-featured chess server. The name Lichess itself is commonly described as a blend of live, light, and libre with chess, reflecting both its real-time nature and its commitment to being free and open software. In 2018, the project's operations were formalized under Lichess, a registered French non-profit association, which today owns and operates the site, employs a small number of paid staff including Duplessis as a full-time developer, and publishes its finances and running costs publicly at lichess.org/costs as part of its stated commitment to transparency as a genuine non-profit rather than merely an ad-free product. Technically, the Lila codebase is a large, multi-language application: the backend is written primarily in Scala using the Play Framework, with the frontend built in TypeScript, additional performance-critical services written in Rust, and other components in Python and Dart, including the mobile app. Data is stored in MongoDB, and the platform's real-time game play relies on WebSocket connections to synchronize moves instantly between players worldwide. The entire codebase, along with the site's visual design, is published as open source, predominantly under the GNU Affero General Public License, with a small number of dependencies under other free or non-free licenses, meaning anyone can inspect, self-host, or contribute to the exact software running Lichess.org, an unusually high degree of transparency for a site with the scale Lichess has reached. As a platform, Lichess offers essentially the full feature set of a modern chess service entirely free, with no advertising and no paywalled functionality of consequence: unlimited real-time and correspondence games at any time control, a full Elo-style rating system across multiple time controls and variants, chess variants beyond standard chess such as Chess960, Crazyhouse, King of the Hill, and Atomic, a built-in analysis board powered by the open-source Stockfish engine, tools for studying openings and annotating games, tournaments including arena and Swiss formats that anyone can create, a puzzle trainer with millions of positions, integrated coaching and streaming features, and a training mode against the open-source Leela Chess Zero neural network engine at adjustable strengths. By mid-2024, the platform had hosted more than 5.6 billion games since launch, and it consistently ranks among the most-visited chess and general gaming websites globally, drawing significant traffic from Russia, the United States, Germany, and Turkey among other countries, according to third-party analytics. There is no pricing model in the conventional sense: every feature on Lichess is available to every user for free, and the association funds its servers, small paid staff, and moderation operations entirely through voluntary donations. Donors who contribute can optionally receive a Patron badge on their profile as a form of public recognition, but this confers no functional or competitive advantage in gameplay, ratings, or access, a deliberate design choice the project repeatedly emphasizes to distinguish itself from freemium competitors. Lichess's principal competitor is Chess.com, the dominant commercial chess platform, which operates a freemium model with a much larger paid membership tier, along with subscription revenue, advertising, and a significantly larger commercial team; other more niche competitors include chess24 and various ICC-style legacy chess servers. Lila and Lichess differentiate themselves almost entirely on the combination of being completely free, open source down to the code running the production site, ad-free, and governed as a non-profit association rather than a venture-backed or privately held company, a positioning that has made it especially popular with players, educators, and developers who value transparency and want to avoid subscription paywalls for core chess-playing and analysis functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lila is the open-source Scala codebase behind Lichess.org, created in 2010 by French programmer Thibault Duplessis as a hobby project that grew into one of the world's largest online chess platforms.

Since 2018, Lichess has operated under a registered French non-profit association, funding a small paid staff and infrastructure entirely through voluntary donations rather than advertising, subscriptions, or venture capital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lichess offers unlimited real-time and correspondence games, a full rating system, chess variants like Chess960 and Crazyhouse, and a built-in Stockfish-powered analysis board, all free with no paywalled tier.

Additional tools include a puzzle trainer with millions of positions, user-created arena and Swiss tournaments, study and annotation tools, and training against the open-source Leela Chess Zero engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Every feature on Lichess is free for every user, with no advertising and no premium tier; the platform is funded entirely by voluntary donations to the non-profit association that operates it.
Donors may optionally receive a Patron badge on their profile as public recognition, but this provides no functional or competitive advantage over non-donating users.

features.title: Unlimited Free Real-Time and Correspondence Games
features.description: Play at any time control with no limits and no subscription requirement.

features.title: Built-In Stockfish Analysis Board
features.description: Analyze any game with the open-source Stockfish engine directly in the browser.

features.title: Chess Variants
features.description: Supports Chess960, Crazyhouse, King of the Hill, Atomic, and other variants.

features.title: Puzzle Trainer
features.description: Millions of tactical puzzles rated to match player skill level.

features.title: Arena and Swiss Tournaments
features.description: Anyone can create and join open tournaments in multiple formats.

features.title: Study Tool
features.description: Collaborative tools for annotating games and studying openings.

features.title: Fully Open-Source Codebase
features.description: The entire production codebase, called Lila, is published under the AGPL license.

features.title: Streaming and Coaching Integration
features.description: Built-in tools for coaches and streamers to broadcast games and lessons.

pros: Completely free with no ads and no paywalled features
Entire production codebase is open source and auditable
Operated transparently as a registered non-profit with published finances
Huge, active player base supporting fast matchmaking at nearly any time control
Strong built-in analysis tools powered by open-source engines like Stockfish and Leela Chess Zero
cons: Smaller catalog of structured video lessons and courses than commercial competitor Chess.com
Interface is more utilitarian and less polished for casual or beginner users than some paid platforms
Relies entirely on donations, which can constrain infrastructure investment versus VC-funded rivals
Smaller professional content and events ecosystem than the market-leading commercial platform
Community-run moderation and support can be slower than a dedicated commercial support team

faqs.question: What is Lila?
faqs.answer: Lila is the name of the open-source software codebase that powers Lichess.org, written primarily in Scala.

faqs.question: Who created Lichess?
faqs.answer: Lichess was created in 2010 by French programmer Thibault Duplessis as a personal side project.

faqs.question: Is Lichess really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lichess is entirely free, ad-free, and funded by voluntary donations through a registered French non-profit association.

faqs.question: Is Lichess open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Lila codebase and the site's design are published as open source, mainly under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: Does donating to Lichess unlock extra features?
faqs.answer: No, donors only receive an optional Patron badge on their profile; all gameplay and analysis features remain the same for everyone.

faqs.question: How does Lichess compare to Chess.com?
faqs.answer: Chess.com is a commercial, venture-backed freemium platform with a larger paid subscriber base, while Lichess is a free, open-source, donation-funded non-profit.

use_cases.title: Free Online Chess Play and Analysis
use_cases.description: Players use Lichess for unlimited free games, ratings, and post-game analysis with Stockfish.

use_cases.title: Chess Education and Coaching
use_cases.description: Coaches and teachers use Lichess's study tools and streaming integration to teach students for free.

use_cases.title: Open-Source Chess Development
use_cases.description: Developers study, self-host, or contribute to the Lila codebase for their own chess projects.
use_cases.audience: Developers building on or contributing to chess software

features: Unlimited games at all time controls
Supports server costs and non-profit operations

enrichment.short_description: All-in-one business banking app offering checking, savings, invoicing and tax tools designed for freelancers and small businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Lili Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lili is an all-in-one business banking app for freelancers and small businesses. Compare its free and paid plans, features, and top fintech alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lili is a fintech company that offers an all-in-one business banking app built specifically for freelancers, sole proprietors, and small business owners, combining a business checking account with built-in bookkeeping, invoicing, and tax-savings tools in a single mobile-first product. The company, legally Lili App Inc., was founded in 2018 and launched publicly in late 2019, headquartered in New York City. It was co-founded by Lilac Bar David, who serves as CEO, and Liran Zelkha, who serves as CTO. Bar David brought fintech and banking experience from Israel's Leumi Card and from co-founding the Tel Aviv-based digital bank Pepper before starting Lili. Lili is a fintech app rather than a chartered bank itself; its banking services, deposit accounts, and FDIC insurance are provided through partner banks in its network, with sources identifying both Choice Financial Group and Sunrise Banks as program banks whose FDIC coverage backs Lili customer deposits, consistent with the common banking-as-a-service structure used by most challenger banking apps in the United States. Lili raised substantial venture funding in its early years: a 10 million dollar seed round in June 2020 led by Group 11 with participation from Foundation Capital, AltaIR Capital, Primary Venture Partners, and Torch Capital; a 15 million dollar Series A just months later in October 2020; and a 55 million dollar Series B in May 2021, bringing total funding to roughly 80 million dollars. By 2024 the company had grown from its two founders to nearly 100 full-time employees and reported serving more than 200,000 freelancers and small business customers. Functionally, Lili centers on a no-monthly-fee business checking account that comes with a Visa business debit card, early direct deposit, and a savings feature currently advertising up to 4.00 percent Annual Percentage Yield with no minimum balance or lock-up period, with deposits spread across a network of FDIC-insured partner banks providing pass-through insurance coverage reported as high as 3 million dollars. Beyond basic banking, Lili's differentiator is bundling small-business financial operations tools directly into the banking app: automatic transaction categorization intended to simplify bookkeeping and tax preparation, built-in invoicing so freelancers can bill clients without a separate tool, bill pay functionality, expedited check deposits, domestic and international wire transfers, and a credit-building program that reports to Dun and Bradstreet to help sole proprietors establish business credit history. Higher tiers add access to business lines of credit up to 250,000 dollars, team collaboration features with role-based account access for businesses with employees or contractors, and integrations with popular small-business software including QuickBooks, Xero, Stripe, Shopify, and Gusto, letting Lili sit at the center of a freelancer's broader financial stack rather than functioning as a standalone checking account. Lili's pricing follows a freemium structure. The base account, sometimes referred to as Lili Basic or Core, carries no monthly fee and includes the checking account, debit card, and savings APY. Above that, Lili Pro is priced at roughly 15 dollars a month and layers in automated bookkeeping and tax-savings tools; Lili Smart, at roughly 35 dollars a month, adds invoicing, bill pay, and team collaboration features; and Lili Premium, at roughly 55 dollars a month, represents the most complete tier, combining the benefits of the lower tiers with the highest savings yield and access to Lili's credit-building and financing tools. All tiers include 24/7 customer support, and the free tier is positioned to be sufficient for a freelancer who only needs a basic no-fee business checking account, while the paid tiers are aimed at businesses that want to consolidate accounting, invoicing, and cash management into the same app they bank with. Lili's target audience is explicitly freelancers, gig workers, sole proprietors, and very small businesses who are often underserved or overcharged by traditional small-business banking products at large incumbent banks, and who want banking, bookkeeping, and tax preparation support without hiring a separate accountant or subscribing to multiple disconnected tools. Its closest competitors in the US neobank-for-small-business category include Novo, Found, Bluevine, and Mercury, which skews toward startups rather than freelancers, along with traditional business checking products from banks like Chase and Bank of America. Lili differentiates itself through its freelancer-first design and heavy emphasis on tax preparation and quarterly tax-savings automation, a feature set explicitly aimed at 1099 contractors and solo entrepreneurs who face more complex self-employment tax obligations than traditional small businesses. It is worth noting that Lili the business banking app should not be confused with the unrelated open-source chess codebase Lila that powers Lichess.org; the two share a similar name but operate in entirely different industries with no corporate relationship.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lili is a fintech company founded in 2018 and launched in late 2019 by CEO Lilac Bar David and CTO Liran Zelkha, offering an all-in-one banking app for freelancers, sole proprietors, and small businesses out of its New York headquarters.

The company raised roughly 80 million dollars across seed, Series A, and Series B rounds between 2020 and 2021 from investors including Group 11, Foundation Capital, and Torch Capital, and now serves more than 200,000 customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lili combines a no-fee business checking account and Visa debit card with a savings account offering up to 4.00 percent APY, plus automatic expense categorization and tax-savings tools for freelancers.

Paid tiers add invoicing, bill pay, team collaboration with role-based access, a Dun and Bradstreet credit-building program, and lines of credit up to 250,000 dollars, alongside integrations with QuickBooks, Xero, Stripe, Shopify, and Gusto.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lili's base account carries no monthly fee and includes checking, a debit card, and savings APY, making it usable at no cost for freelancers who need only basic banking.
Paid tiers scale up from roughly 15 dollars a month for Lili Pro to 35 dollars for Lili Smart and 55 dollars for Lili Premium, each layering in more bookkeeping, invoicing, and financing features.

features.title: No-Fee Business Checking
features.description: Business checking account with a Visa debit card and no monthly fee on the base tier.

features.title: High-Yield Business Savings
features.description: Savings account advertising up to 4.00 percent APY with no minimum balance.

features.title: Automatic Expense and Tax Categorization
features.description: Transactions are automatically categorized to simplify bookkeeping and tax prep.

features.title: Built-In Invoicing and Bill Pay
features.description: Send invoices and pay bills directly from the banking app.

features.title: Business Credit Building
features.description: Reports payment history to Dun and Bradstreet to help build business credit.

features.title: Lines of Credit
features.description: Eligible customers can access business lines of credit up to 250,000 dollars.

features.title: Team Collaboration
features.description: Role-based access lets teams share account access securely.

features.title: Software Integrations
features.description: Connects with QuickBooks, Xero, Stripe, Shopify, and Gusto.

pros: Free base tier with no monthly fees or minimum balance requirements
Combines banking, bookkeeping and tax-savings tools in a single app
Competitive savings APY compared to many traditional small-business banks
Purpose-built for freelancers and 1099 contractors, including tax-specific features
Backed by well-known venture investors with roughly 80 million dollars in funding raised
cons: Not a chartered bank itself, so account holders rely on a partner-bank structure for FDIC coverage
Advanced bookkeeping, invoicing and credit tools require paid tiers up to 55 dollars a month
No physical branches, which may not suit business owners who prefer in-person banking
Lines of credit and financing require qualification and are not guaranteed to all users
Smaller product ecosystem than larger business banking platforms like Mercury or Bluevine for scaling companies

faqs.question: Is Lili a bank?
faqs.answer: No, Lili is a fintech company; banking services and FDIC insurance are provided through partner banks in its network.

faqs.question: How much does Lili cost?
faqs.answer: Lili's base account has no monthly fee, with paid tiers Lili Pro, Smart and Premium priced at roughly 15, 35 and 55 dollars a month respectively.

faqs.question: Who founded Lili?
faqs.answer: Lili was founded in 2018 by CEO Lilac Bar David and CTO Liran Zelkha, and launched publicly in late 2019.

faqs.question: What savings interest rate does Lili offer?
faqs.answer: Lili has advertised savings yields up to 4.00 percent APY with no minimum balance requirement.

faqs.question: Does Lili offer business credit or loans?
faqs.answer: Yes, higher tiers offer access to business lines of credit up to 250,000 dollars along with a credit-building program that reports to Dun and Bradstreet.

faqs.question: Who is Lili designed for?
faqs.answer: Lili is designed primarily for freelancers, gig workers, sole proprietors and small business owners.

use_cases.title: Freelancer and Gig Worker Banking
use_cases.description: Freelancers use Lili's free checking account and tax tools to manage income and expenses in one place.

use_cases.title: Small Business Bookkeeping and Tax Prep
use_cases.description: Sole proprietors use Lili Pro or Smart to automatically categorize expenses and prepare for tax season.
use_cases.audience: Sole proprietors managing their own books

use_cases.title: Small Team Financial Management
use_cases.description: Small businesses use Lili Smart or Premium for team collaboration with role-based account access.
use_cases.audience: Small businesses needing shared account access

features: Business checking account with Visa debit card
No monthly fees or minimum balance
FDIC insurance through partner bank network
